askNext: Is the audience being told that this persona is synthetic?
Who owns the likeness, voice, backstory, and moderation policy?
What real human claim is the brand trying to borrow without a real human?
synthetic-influencer.body: The synthetic influencer is less a person than a content calendar with cheekbones.
synthetic-influencer.example: "The synthetic influencer loved the skincare range, partly because the campaign brief had not modelled acne, ageing, or refusing the fee."
synthetic-influencer.misuse: It gets abused when a designed persona is treated as authentic community presence. A synthetic influencer can perform a style and hold an audience, but it cannot honestly live a life, disclose a private experience, or provide lived endorsement.
synthetic-influencer.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brand governance, disclosure rules, likeness rights, synthetic content labelling, moderation, audience trust, and a careful answer to who is responsible when the invented person says something expensive.

translations.label: marketing meaning
translations.text: A controlled digital persona used to attract attention and sell things.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A cheaper, safer-looking ambassador whose risks have moved from behaviour to governance.

synthetic-influencer.warningLabel: A fictional spokesperson still needs real disclosure and real accountability.
synthetic-influencer.whyExists: It exists because brands want repeatable personalities that can be localised, scheduled, licensed, and revised without negotiating with a human being who has opinions, agents, fatigue, or old tweets.

askNext: What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place?
Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers?
Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives?
synthetic-user.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.
synthetic-user.example: "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
synthetic-user.misuse: It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
synthetic-user.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

synthetic-user.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.
synthetic-user.whyExists: It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.

askNext: Which real evidence seeded these synthetic participants?
Are we using this to generate hypotheses or to replace contact with customers?
What decision would change if real users disagree with the simulation?
synthetic-user-research.body: Synthetic user research is most honest when it admits the word "research" is doing some supervised stretching.
synthetic-user-research.example: "The synthetic user research said the pricing page was clear, which was comforting until actual users reached the part involving money."
synthetic-user-research.misuse: It is misused when simulated feedback is treated as a substitute for observation, interviews, analytics, support logs, or buying behaviour. A model can reflect patterns in its training data and prompt. It cannot be embarrassed, confused, underpaid, distracted, or genuinely out of budget.
synthetic-user-research.practicalMeaning: In practice it belongs near discovery planning, early concept testing, assumption mapping, and interview preparation. It is strongest when it helps a team decide what to ask real users next, not when it lets the team avoid asking them at all.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Using simulated participants to explore likely reactions before or alongside real research.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fast assumption generator with a remarkable ability to sound like validation.

synthetic-user-research.warningLabel: Useful for hypotheses. Dangerous as a replacement for evidence.
synthetic-user-research.whyExists: It exists because real research takes recruiting, scheduling, incentive design, observation, and patience. Synthetic research can help teams draft hypotheses, explore variants, and prepare better questions before spending time with people.

askNext: What exact meaning of System card is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
system-card.body: System card gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Claude Mythos Preview keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
system-card.example: "Once system card entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
system-card.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
system-card.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

system-card.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead?
How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions?
What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed?
system-prompt.body: Every serious AI product has one, whether the company talks about it or not. The interesting part is never merely that it exists. The interesting part is what else the team expects it to do.
system-prompt.example: "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
system-prompt.misuse: By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
system-prompt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

system-prompt.warningLabel: An instruction layer, not a security model.
system-prompt.whyExists: Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
